steps.title: Pin down what the underlined claim needs
steps.content: The underlined sentence asserts that even in countries where almost everyone speaks Swahili, many of those speakers learned it as a second, third, or fourth language — not as a first language. Supporting evidence must show that the total Swahili-speaking population in such a country greatly exceeds the worldwide first-language total (about 15 million) cited in the passage.

steps.title: Match the data to that need
steps.content: Choice A — Tanzania has approximately 61 million Swahili speakers, which is much more than the estimated total number of people worldwide for whom Swahili is their first language. reports that Tanzania has roughly 61 million Swahili speakers, which is far more than the ~15 million worldwide first-language total. Because Tanzania alone has many more Swahili speakers than there are first-language speakers globally, most Tanzanian speakers must have learned Swahili as a non-first language — exactly the claim.

steps.title: Eliminate the other choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Tanzania has approximately 61 million Swahili speakers, which is much more than the estimated total number of people worldwide for whom Swahili is their first language.: directly demonstrates the gap the claim requires. ✓
  • Choice B — Tanzania is estimated to have at most 15 million Swahili speakers, while the country's total population is approximately 61 million people.: misreads the table and fails to show that total speakers exceed first-language speakers. ✗
  • Choice C — Approximately 100 percent of the people who speak Swahili as their first language live in Kenya, which has a total population of approximately 55 million people.: an overreach the table does not support, and it does not address non-first-language acquisition. ✗
  • Choice D — Approximately 100 percent of Kenya's population speaks Swahili, while only about 25 percent of the Democratic Republic of the Congo's population speaks Swahili.: compares two countries' speaker percentages but never contrasts total speakers with first-language speakers, so it cannot support the claim. ✗
That matches Choice A — Tanzania has approximately 61 million Swahili speakers, which is much more than the estimated total number of people worldwide for whom Swahili is their first language..
